name: Salem Auto Body
description: Established in 1924, Salem Auto Body is a family-owned and operated business dedicated to providing high-quality collision repair services. From windshield replacements to comprehensive body work and electrical service, they handle a wide range of auto body needs. The business has been passed down through three generations of the Lupo family, building a legacy of integrity and customer service in Salem.
short_narration: Salem Auto Body has been serving the Salem community since 1924, offering expert collision repair and auto body services passed down through three generations of the Lupo family.
long_narration:
Salem Auto Body has been a fixture of the Salem, Massachusetts community since 1924. Founded on a commitment to quality and customer care, the business has been passed down through three generations of the Lupo family, each building upon the last’s dedication to expert auto body repair.

Starting with Thomas “Tommy” Lupo in the 1950s, then his son Jeffrey, and now Corrin Lupo, Salem Auto Body has adapted to the changing landscape of automotive technology while maintaining its core values. Corrin Lupo opened Salem Auto Body LLC in 2021, investing in a modern, technologically advanced facility to provide the latest in collision repair techniques.

Today, Salem Auto Body offers a full range of services, including collision repair, windshield replacement, suspension work, and electrical service. Their commitment to honesty and excellence ensures that every customer receives personalized attention and a repair they can trust.

name: Count Orlok’s Nightmare Gallery
description: Bigger, better and ever-expanding, Salem’s TOP RATED and ONLY Horror Museum, filled with life-sized creatures and characters from 100+ years by Hollywood SPFX artists! A unique must-stop for all genre fans! Gift shop on-site!
short_narration: Count Orlok's Nightmare Gallery is at 217 Essex Street
long_narration:
Count Orlok's Nightmare Gallery at 217 Essex Street is Salem's
only horror museum, and it has consistently earned top ratings
from visitors. The gallery features life-sized creatures and
characters spanning over a hundred years of horror cinema, created
by Hollywood special effects artists. Expect to encounter classics
from Universal Monsters to modern horror icons, each rendered with
movie-quality detail and artistry. The collection is ever-expanding,
with new additions appearing regularly. The name references Count
Orlok, the vampire from F.W. Murnau's 1922 silent film Nosferatu,
one of the foundational works of horror cinema. The choice reflects
the gallery's focus on horror as an art form, not just cheap scares.
This is a museum for genre enthusiasts who appreciate the
craftsmanship behind the creatures. A gift shop on-site offers
horror-themed merchandise. The gallery is located on Essex Street's
pedestrian mall, making it easy to combine with visits to Salem's
witch shops, psychic parlors, and other attractions. This is one
of Salem's best rainy-day options.

name: Essex Heritage Tours
description: Essex Heritage Tours provides a variety of ways to discover the rich history and heritage of Salem and the Essex River. From guided walking tours through historic neighborhoods to relaxing boat tours along the coastline, and informative historical films, they offer something for every visitor. Located at 2 New Liberty Street, they are open Wednesday through Sunday from 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM.
short_narration: Explore Salem and the Essex River with Essex Heritage Tours, offering engaging walking tours, scenic boat tours, and insightful historical films.
long_narration:
Essex Heritage Tours serves as a gateway to the fascinating history and natural beauty of Salem and the Essex River. Dedicated to preserving and sharing the region’s maritime legacy, they offer a diverse range of experiences, including meticulously researched walking tours that bring Salem’s past to life, scenic boat tours that showcase the stunning Essex River and coastline, and captivating historical films that delve into the area’s unique stories. 

Founded to promote awareness of the Essex National Heritage Area, Essex Heritage Tours connects visitors with the cultural landscape shaped by centuries of maritime trade, shipbuilding, and innovation. Their knowledgeable guides and thoughtfully curated tours provide a deeper understanding of Salem’s role in American history and the enduring connection between the community and the sea. 

Located at 2 New Liberty Street, Essex Heritage Tours is open Wednesday through Sunday, offering a convenient starting point for exploring the many historical treasures of Salem and the Essex River region. With accessible parking and a commitment to providing enriching experiences, they welcome visitors of all ages and interests.
historical_note: Essex Heritage Tours, based in Salem, Massachusetts, is dedicated to preserving and sharing the history and heritage of the Essex River and surrounding communities. They offer a range of experiences, including guided walking tours of Salem's historic neighborhoods, boat tours exploring the river's maritime legacy, and educational historical films. With a commitment to accessibility, including wheelchair-accessible parking, Essex Heritage Tours welcomes visitors to delve into the rich cultural tapestry of this historic region.

name: Fame of Salem Sailboat Tour
description: The *Fame of Salem* is a beautifully restored 1894 schooner offering a unique perspective on Salem's rich maritime history. Departing from Derby Wharf, the *Fame* offers daily sails, sunset cruises, and private charters. Step aboard and experience the harbor as Salem's seafaring ancestors did, enjoying views of the historic waterfront and learning about the city's pivotal role in global trade.
short_narration: Experience Salem from the water aboard the *Fame of Salem*, a historic 19th-century schooner offering daily sails and sunset cruises.
long_narration:
The *Fame of Salem* is more than just a scenic cruise; it’s a journey back in time. This meticulously restored 1894 schooner embodies Salem’s legacy as a major maritime center, harkening back to the era when ships like the *Fame* sailed the globe, bringing wealth and exotic goods to the city’s wharves.

Originally built as a racing vessel, the *Fame* has been lovingly restored to her former glory, offering visitors an authentic sailing experience. Departing from Derby Wharf, the cruises provide stunning views of Salem’s historic waterfront, including Fort Pickering and Castle Island. Knowledgeable crew members share stories of Salem’s maritime past, from the clipper ships and privateers to the merchants and sailors who built the city’s fortune.

Whether you choose a daytime sail or a romantic sunset cruise, a voyage on the *Fame of Salem* is an unforgettable way to connect with the city’s history and enjoy the beauty of its harbor.

short_narration: Each October, the Festival of the Dead brings a unique exploration of death’s customs and rituals to Salem, hosted by local Warlocks Christian Day and Brian Cain.
long_narration:
The Festival of the Dead is an annual event series in Salem that invites guests to explore the history, customs, and rituals surrounding death. Founded by Salem Warlocks Christian Day and Brian Cain, the festival offers a unique perspective on humanity’s relationship with mortality, delving into the ways different cultures have honored and understood the afterlife.

Events range from psychic readings and séances to historical presentations and theatrical performances, all designed to create an immersive and thought-provoking experience. The festival’s location on Essex Street places it in the heart of Salem’s historic district, surrounded by shops and attractions that reflect the city’s rich occult and maritime past.

The Festival of the Dead has become a popular draw for visitors interested in witchcraft, the paranormal, and Salem’s unique spiritual heritage, offering a different kind of experience than the city’s more traditional haunted attractions.

long_narration: Witchwood Tours offers a unique perspective on Salem’s history and natural landscape. Founded in September 2020 by Euni Morrow, the company fulfills a long-held dream of connecting people with the local ecosystem and sharing a philosophy rooted in nature and folklore. Their signature Forest Lore Tour is a 75-minute walk through Salem Woods, delving into the history, sustainability, and magical aspects of the forest.\n\nThe tours are led by a practicing Witch, offering a unique and informed perspective on the intersection of science, history, and folklore. Beyond the guided walks, Witchwood Tours also hosts workshops, teaching skills like herbalism, felting, and the creation of natural pigments, further immersing participants in the world of sustainable living and traditional crafts. Witchwood Tours has garnered attention from publications like CW’s Mysteries Decoded and Elle Magazine (France), solidifying its place as a distinctive attraction in Salem.

name: Gallows Hill Museum/Theatre
short_narration: Gallows Hill Museum and Theatre is at 7 Lynde Street
long_narration:
Gallows Hill Museum and Theatre at 7 Lynde Street combines museum
exhibits with live theatrical performance to tell the story of
Salem's supernatural past. The location on Lynde Street places it
near several other witch trial attractions, creating a cluster of
experiences that visitors can explore on foot. The name Gallows
Hill evokes one of Salem's most potent and haunting images. For
centuries, a hill by that name was believed to be where the accused
witches were hanged in 1692. The actual execution site was
confirmed in 2016 as Proctor's Ledge, a different location. But
the power of the name endures. The museum component features
exhibits on the witch trials and Salem's broader history of the
supernatural. The theater element adds live performance, bringing
an immediacy that static exhibits cannot match. The combination
makes for an experience that is both educational and visceral.
Check with the venue for current show times and pricing. Phone
978-825-0222.

name: Gallows Hill Salem
description: Gallows Hill Salem is a popular attraction offering a unique and immersive experience of Salem's spectral past. Through a 30-minute live show featuring history, lore, and special effects, visitors are transported back in time. The attraction also includes 'The Lost Museum' walk-through, a trolley tour exploring Salem's ghostly legends, and after-hours ghost hunts. ‘Black Mary Widow’ is a theatrical character created by Gallows Hill Museum & Theater for their tours; she is not a documented historical figure of Salem or the 1692 witch trials.
short_narration: Gallows Hill Salem offers a theatrical retelling of Salem's history, blending the 1692 witch trials with local lore and ghostly tales.
long_narration:
Gallows Hill Salem provides a captivating journey into Salem’s haunted history, blending historical fact with local legends and ghostly tales. The attraction’s centerpiece is a 30-minute live show that uses special effects and storytelling to immerse visitors in the atmosphere of 17th-century Salem and the events of the 1692 witch trials. Beyond the main show, Gallows Hill offers a variety of ways to explore Salem’s darker side, including ‘The Lost Museum’—a walk-through exhibit of oddities and curiosities—and the ‘Ghosts + Legends Trolley’ tour, which explores the city’s most haunted locations.  For those seeking a truly chilling experience, after-hours ghost hunts are also available. The attraction is known for its immersive experience, surpassing other attractions in town and providing a dreamlike view of Salem’s past.

It’s important to note that ‘Black Mary Widow’ is a theatrical character created by Gallows Hill Museum & Theater for their tours; she is not a documented historical figure of Salem or the 1692 witch trials. This highlights the attraction’s commitment to both historical accuracy and engaging entertainment.
historical_note: You’re standing where Salem’s darkest stories come to life, though not in the way you might expect. This isn’t hallowed ground from 1692, but a place where the echoes of that year are deliberately summoned. Gallows Hill Salem isn’t built on the site of the actual hangings – that was a different location, on what’s now Proctor Street – but it *is* built on the power of that story. The attraction’s creators have crafted a theatrical experience, a dreamlike retelling of the trials, using special effects and a little bit of magic. They’ve even created a character, ‘Black Mary Widow,’ to guide you through the lore. She’s not a documented historical figure, but a theatrical creation meant to embody the spirit of the accused. As you look at the building, remember that Salem’s history is always being reinterpreted, reimagined, and retold. The stories change, but the questions they raise about fear, justice, and the power of belief remain as potent as ever.

name: Martial Arts Research Institute
description: The Martial Arts Research Institute, established in 2004 by May Williams, is dedicated to the study and practice of Indonesian, Filipino, and Chinese martial arts. Specializing in styles like Pencak Silat, Escrima, and Pin Sun Wing Chun, the institute provides instruction in self-defense, weapon-based combat, and internal energy cultivation through Chi Gong. Located at 53 Mason Street in Salem, the institute welcomes students of all levels.
short_narration: Founded in 2004, the Martial Arts Research Institute offers training in a diverse range of Indonesian, Filipino, and Chinese martial arts.
long_narration:
The Martial Arts Research Institute, established in 2004, is a unique Salem business dedicated to the study and practice of diverse martial arts traditions. Owner May Williams has cultivated a school specializing in Indonesian (Pencak Silat, Harimau, Kendang), Filipino (Escrima, Kali, Arnis), and Chinese (Kuntau, Pin Sun Wing Chun) systems, offering students a comprehensive education in self-defense, physical fitness, and cultural heritage.

Unlike many modern martial arts schools, the Institute emphasizes authentic techniques and lineages. Students can explore the powerful movements of Indonesian Silat, the weapon-based combat of Filipino Kali, and the internal energy work of Chinese Chi Gong. The school's commitment to preserving these traditions makes it a distinctive destination for martial arts enthusiasts in the Salem area.

The Institute is located at 53 Mason Street, offering a dedicated space for training and practice. With a focus on quality instruction and a welcoming atmosphere, the Martial Arts Research Institute provides a path for students of all levels to develop their skills and deepen their understanding of these ancient arts.

name: Real Pirates Salem
description: Real Pirates Salem offers a unique glimpse into the world of 18th-century piracy, displaying over 300 original artifacts recovered from the Whydah Gally, a vessel captained by the infamous “Black Sam” Bellamy. Founder Barry Clifford spent years searching for the wreck, driven by childhood tales of pirate treasure. Today, visitors can explore the recovered riches and learn the stories of the pirates who sailed aboard.
short_narration: Real Pirates Salem showcases authentic treasure and artifacts recovered from the Whydah Gally, a pirate ship lost off the coast of Cape Cod nearly 300 years ago.
long_narration:
Real Pirates Salem brings to life the story of the Whydah Gally, a pirate ship lost off the coast of Cape Cod in 1717. Founder Barry Clifford, captivated by local legends from childhood, dedicated his life to locating the wreck, and in 1984, his persistence paid off. The museum now displays over 300 authentic artifacts recovered from the ship – including coins, jewelry, weapons, and personal belongings – offering a rare and intimate look into the lives of 18th-century pirates.\n
The Whydah Gally was commanded by “Black Sam” Bellamy, known as one of the most successful and youngest pirates of all time. The museum also tells the story of Maria Hallett, Bellamy’s lover, and the complex relationship that shaped his life at sea. Real Pirates Salem is more than just a collection of artifacts; it’s a window into a forgotten world of adventure, danger, and untold riches.
historical_note: Real Pirates Salem offers a unique glimpse into the world of 18th-century piracy, displaying over 300 authentic artifacts recovered from the Whydah Gally, a pirate ship wrecked off the coast of Cape Cod in 1717. Founded by explorer Barry Clifford, the museum brings to life the story of “Black Sam” Bellamy and his crew, revealing a fascinating history of adventure, treasure, and maritime life. It's a family-friendly destination offering a captivating experience for all ages.

name: Sail Salem
description: Sail Salem is a non-profit organization dedicated to empowering individuals through sailing education. Founded in 2007, it continues a tradition of community sailing programs sponsored by the Salem Park, Recreation & Community Services Department. Located at 50 Winter Island Road, Sail Salem offers youth and adult lessons, fostering independence, self-confidence, and community spirit.
short_narration: Sail Salem, established in 2007, provides accessible sailing programs from its base at Winter Island, building on a legacy of community sailing in Salem.
long_narration:
Sail Salem is a non-profit organization dedicated to making sailing accessible to the Salem community. Established in 2007, the organization grew out of long-standing community sailing programs sponsored by the Salem Park, Recreation & Community Services Department, ensuring a continuous tradition of maritime education.

Located at 50 Winter Island Road, Sail Salem offers a range of programs for both youth and adults, from introductory lessons to advanced sailing camps. The organization's mission is to foster independence, self-confidence, and a sense of community through the sport of sailing. Sail Salem is supported by a dedicated volunteer board and receives funding from the city of Salem and the U.S. Department of Housing & Urban Development.

Winter Island itself has a rich maritime history, and Sail Salem’s location reflects that heritage. The organization's commitment to providing affordable and inclusive sailing opportunities ensures that the joy of being on the water is available to everyone in the Salem area.
historical_note: You're looking at a piece of Salem's aviation history. This building, now home to Sail Salem, wasn't always about sails. It was built in 1934 as part of a US Coast Guard Air Station, one of the first in New England. Imagine seaplanes taking off from the harbor, right where the sailboats are now. This wasn't just a base for rescue missions; it was a vital part of coastal defense. The garage you see before you was where those planes were maintained, kept ready to patrol the Atlantic. In 1994, it was recognized for its historical significance and added to the National Register of Historic Places. Today, Sail Salem carries on a different kind of maritime tradition, teaching people of all ages to harness the wind and explore the harbor. But if you look closely at the structure, you can still sense the echoes of propellers and the dedication of the Coast Guard pilots who once called this place home.

Currently, the gallery is preparing for a 2026 exhibit focused on the historical concept of witch hunts and moral panics, inviting artists to submit work that reflects these themes.\n\nThe gallery regularly features both established and emerging artists, including digital artist Jessica Wood (Rustic Bones) and insect taxidermist Adam Inzalaco (Dead Leaf Designs). Beyond visual art, the Salem Art Gallery also hosts ceremonies, including weddings and unbaptisms, offering a space for alternative rituals and celebrations. It’s a destination that challenges conventional notions of art, religion, and community.
historical_note: You're standing on Bridge Street, a thoroughfare that has connected Salem to the harbor for centuries. While the building you see before you is relatively modern, its purpose speaks to a very old current in this town: the exploration of the unseen. Today, it houses the Salem Art Gallery, part of The Satanic Temple, and a space dedicated to art that challenges perceptions and confronts difficult themes. They’re currently preparing an exhibit for 2026, seeking works inspired by witch hunts and moral panics – a fitting subject for Salem, a place forever marked by the events of 1692. The gallery isn’t just a display space; it’s a venue for ceremonies, even weddings and unbaptisms, and currently features the work of artists like Jessica Wood, whose pieces explore the darker side of nature. name: Salem Trolley
description: Established in 1982, Salem Trolley provides narrated one-hour tours through historic Salem, Massachusetts. short_narration: Since 1982, Salem Trolley has offered narrated one-hour tours of Salem, covering eight miles of historic landmarks and sharing the city’s unique stories.
long_narration:
For over four decades, Salem Trolley has been a beloved way to experience the history and charm of Salem. Founded in 1982, the company quickly became known as Salem’s Original Trolley Tour, setting the standard for guided sightseeing in the Witch City.

The one-hour narrated tours cover eight miles, providing a comprehensive overview of Salem’s most important landmarks. Passengers will pass the historic Charter Street Burial Ground, the literary landmark of the House of the Seven Gables, and the museums dedicated to the 1692 witch trials, including the Witch Dungeon Museum and Salem Witch Museum. The tour also explores the waterfront and the elegant homes of Chestnut Street, offering a glimpse into Salem’s maritime past.

Today, Salem Trolley continues to offer a convenient and informative way to explore Salem, with accessible vehicles and knowledgeable guides dedicated to sharing the city’s stories with visitors.

Owned and operated by Tammy Harrington, Salem Trolley remains a popular way to experience the Witch City.

name: Salem Witch Museum
description: The Salem Witch Museum, established in 1972, has welcomed millions of visitors seeking to understand the tragic events of the 1692 witch trials. Through immersive exhibits featuring life-size stage sets and compelling narratives, the museum recreates the atmosphere of 17th-century Salem and explores the broader history of witchcraft and persecution. It continues to be a central destination for those interested in this pivotal moment in American history.
short_narration: Founded in 1972, the Salem Witch Museum uses life-size stage sets and dramatic narration to tell the story of the 1692 witch trials and explore the history of witchcraft.
long_narration:
The Salem Witch Museum opened its doors in 1972, quickly becoming a cornerstone of Salem’s historical landscape. Founded to educate visitors about the infamous 1692 witch trials, the museum utilizes a unique approach: large-scale stage sets and dramatic narration to immerse guests in the world of colonial Salem. These exhibits vividly depict the accusations, investigations, and trials that led to the deaths of twenty innocent people.

Beyond the 1692 events, the museum also explores the broader history of witchcraft, from its European roots to its evolving perceptions in modern times. The second exhibit, “Witches: Evolving Perceptions,” examines the historical context of witchcraft accusations and the themes of persecution and scapegoating that continue to resonate today.

For over fifty years, the Salem Witch Museum has served as a vital resource for understanding this complex and often misunderstood period of American history, offering a thought-provoking experience for visitors from around the globe.
historical_note: The Salem Witch Museum, established in 1972, has welcomed millions of visitors seeking to understand the history of the 1692 Salem witch trials. Through dramatic presentations featuring life-size stage sets, the museum recreates the atmosphere of the era and explores the events that unfolded. A second exhibit examines the broader history of witchcraft and persecution.

Rejecting supernatural interpretations, these tours emphasize a scientific and philosophical approach to understanding witchcraft, the 1692 witch trials, and the historical context of religious persecution. Led by experienced local guides—including historians and an Ordained Member of The Satanic Temple—the tours aim to reveal the underlying spirit of rebellion against injustice that has long been a part of Salem’s story.  Founded by Thomas O'Brien Vallor, Satanic Salem Walking Tours provides a thought-provoking and historically grounded experience for those seeking a different kind of Salem tour.

name: Schooner Fame of Salem
description: The Schooner Fame of Salem is a meticulously crafted replica of an 1812 privateer, offering visitors a unique opportunity to experience life at sea. Departing from Pickering Wharf, the Schooner Fame provides sails 3-4 times daily, allowing guests to participate in raising the sails, hear the boom of the cannon, and learn about Salem’s rich maritime history. This engaging tour is a popular way to see Salem from the water and connect with its seafaring past.
short_narration: Experience Salem's maritime past aboard the Schooner Fame, a replica of an 1812 privateer offering hands-on sailing adventures from Pickering Wharf.
long_narration: The Schooner Fame of Salem isn't just a boat tour; it's a journey back in time to Salem’s golden age of maritime commerce. This full-scale replica of an 1812 privateer allows visitors to step aboard and experience what life was like for sailors during the War of 1812. Departing from Pickering Wharf, the Schooner Fame offers daily sails where guests can actively participate in raising the sails, learning nautical skills, and even hearing the roar of a historically accurate cannon.\n\nDuring the early 19th century, Salem was one of the most important ports in the United States, and privateers like the original Fame played a vital role in protecting American shipping and disrupting enemy trade. The replica allows visitors to connect with this exciting and often dangerous period of Salem’s history. The Schooner Fame is a popular attraction for families, history buffs, and anyone looking for a unique and memorable experience on the water.
historical_note: The Schooner Fame is a meticulously crafted replica of an 1812 privateer, offering visitors a unique opportunity to sail Salem's waters and learn about the city's rich maritime past. Departing from Pickering Wharf, the Schooner Fame provides a hands-on experience, allowing guests to participate in raising the sails and even witness a cannon firing. It’s a popular attraction for families and anyone interested in Salem’s seafaring heritage.

short_narration: Step back in time at the Witch Dungeon Museum, where live reenactments bring the drama of the 1692 Salem witch trials to life alongside tours of a historically-inspired dungeon.
long_narration:
The Witch Dungeon Museum provides a dramatic and immersive experience of the 1692 Salem witch trials. Unlike many sites in Salem that focus on the victims or the broader social context, the Witch Dungeon focuses on the courtroom proceedings themselves. Live actors recreate scenes from the trials, bringing to life the accusations, testimonies, and mounting hysteria that gripped the town.\n
Following the reenactment, visitors descend into a replica of a 17th-century dungeon, built to reflect the conditions in which the accused were held while awaiting trial. Though not a historically accurate recreation of a specific dungeon, it aims to convey the claustrophobia and fear experienced by those imprisoned during that time. The museum is open seasonally, from April through November, offering a chilling and thought-provoking experience for visitors interested in Salem's most infamous period.

Visitors can witness live reenactments of the infamous trials, followed by guided tours through a replica 17th-century dungeon. name: Witch History Museum
description: The Witch History Museum offers a compelling look at the events surrounding the 1692 Salem witch trials. short_narration: Step back in time at the Witch History Museum, where life in 1692 Salem comes alive through detailed dioramas and first-person accounts of the infamous witch trials.
long_narration:
The Witch History Museum stands as a dedicated space to understanding the events of 1692, a year forever etched in Salem’s history. Unlike many attractions that focus on the sensational aspects of the witch trials, this museum aims to present a balanced and informative account of the hysteria that consumed the community. Visitors are guided through a series of detailed dioramas, each depicting a scene from the trials and the lives of those accused. 

Through first-person narrations, the museum brings to life the stories of the nineteen individuals who were tragically put to death, offering a glimpse into their lives and the accusations they faced. The museum strives to provide context, exploring the social, religious, and political factors that contributed to the trials, and the lasting impact they had on Salem and beyond. It is a somber, but essential, stop for anyone seeking to understand this pivotal moment in American history.

description: Bill and Bob's Roast Beef, located at 2 Central Street, has been a Peabody favorite since 1968. long_narration: Bill and Bob's Roast Beef has been a cornerstone of the Salem-area dining scene since 1968. Nondas Lagonakis, a Greek immigrant, arrived in Massachusetts in 1961 and worked tirelessly for seven years before realizing his dream of owning a restaurant. He purchased Bill Aylward's existing establishment, and with the dedication of his wife, Ada, transformed it into the beloved local eatery it is today.\n\nNondas and Ada Lagonakis focused on quality and community, building a loyal following over the decades. Their commitment extended to the restaurant's signature sauce: they collaborated on the creation of James River Barbeque sauce, a unique flavor that remains exclusive to Bill and Bob's. The restaurant continues to operate with the same dedication to fresh food and a welcoming atmosphere, making it a Salem-area institution.

description: Coffee Time Bake Shop, located at 96 Bridge Street, has been a Salem staple since 1978, evolving from a donut shop to a full-service bakery. historical_note: You're standing on Bridge Street, a thoroughfare that’s seen Salem change from a bustling seaport to the modern city it is today. Coffee Time Bake Shop is a designated National Register property, recognizing its contribution to the commercial fabric of Salem. It began as the “Donut Ring” in 1978, and in 1988, the owners rebranded and expanded, moving beyond donuts to offer cakes, pastries, and the kind of breakfast that fuels a busy harbor town. Look at the storefront; it’s a simple, functional design, typical of the mid-century. But imagine the smells drifting out the door – warm sugar, baking bread, the first coffee of the day. description: Longboards Restaurant & Bar, located at 76 Wharf Street, has a surprisingly humble beginning. name: Rockafella's Restaurant
description: The Daniel Low building at 231 Essex Street has served many purposes since its construction in 1826. First built for Salem’s First Church, the building later housed a variety of businesses, including shops owned by notable Salem figures like William Bowditch. In the 1860s and 70s, the National Exchange Bank occupied the space, adding a vault that remains visible today. Now known as Rockafella’s Restaurant, the building continues to be a vibrant part of Salem’s dining scene.
short_narration: Originally built as Salem’s First Church in 1826, the Daniel Low building now houses Rockafella’s Restaurant, retaining architectural details like a historic bank vault.
long_narration:
The building at 231 Essex Street has a long and fascinating history, beginning in 1826 when it was constructed as Salem’s First Church. The cornerstone was laid with a silver plate listing the church’s ministers, a testament to the congregation’s deep roots in the community. For decades, the church used the upper floor for worship while leasing the ground floor to commercial ventures.

Throughout the 19th century, a succession of businesses occupied the ground floor, including the crockery shop of renowned mathematician and navigator William Bowditch, and dry goods stores owned by Edwin Ide and John P. Peabody. In the mid-19th century, the National Exchange Bank took over the space, adding a vault and distinctive double-depth windows that are still visible today, a reminder of Salem’s bustling financial history.

Today, the building is home to Rockafella’s Restaurant, established in 2003. The owners have preserved many of the building’s historic features, creating a unique atmosphere that blends the past and present. Rockafella’s continues the tradition of welcoming the community, offering a place to gather, dine, and enjoy Salem’s vibrant energy.
historical_note: Look up at this building – it’s held a congregation, a bank, and now, a lively restaurant. The Daniel Low building, as it’s known, was originally built for Salem’s First Church in 1826. Imagine the scene on April 27th of that year: members of the church laying the cornerstone, and placing a silver plate beneath it, inscribed with the names of their ministers. For decades, the church held services on the second floor, while the ground floor bustled with commerce. William Bowditch, the famous mathematician and navigator, once kept a crockery shop right here. Later, Edwin Ide sold dry goods, and John P. Peabody furnished the ladies of Salem. In the 1860s, the National Exchange Bank moved in, adding the vault and those deep-set windows you see today – they’re still there. If you lean in close, you can almost hear the clink of coins and the rustle of silk. Now, of course, it’s Rockafella’s, but the bones of Salem’s past are still very much alive within these walls.

Located atop The Hotel Salem, this seasonal bar and restaurant provides a relaxed yet sophisticated atmosphere with panoramic views of Salem’s historic streets and vibrant harbor. The menu features a Mexican-inspired selection of tacos, rice bowls, and shareable plates, alongside a curated list of craft cocktails and drinks.\n

name: Lydia Pinkham Memorial Clinic
description: Located at 250 Derby Street, the Lydia Pinkham Memorial Clinic honors the legacy of Lydia Pinkham, a pioneering entrepreneur who created and marketed a widely-used tonic for women’s health in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Pinkham’s Vegetable Compound became a household name, and her marketing strategies were innovative for the time. The clinic carries on her commitment to women’s wellness, offering services and resources to the Salem community.
short_narration: The Lydia Pinkham Memorial Clinic on Derby Street continues the legacy of Lydia Pinkham, a 19th-century entrepreneur who developed and marketed a popular patent medicine for women's health.
long_narration:
The Lydia Pinkham Memorial Clinic stands as a testament to the remarkable story of Lydia Pinkham, a Salem entrepreneur who became a national icon in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Pinkham developed and marketed 'Lydia E. Pinkham’s Vegetable Compound,' a patent medicine marketed to women for a wide range of ailments. While the Compound’s efficacy is debated by modern standards, Pinkham’s savvy marketing—including testimonials and direct correspondence with customers—built a massive brand and made her one of the first successful female entrepreneurs in America.

Pinkham’s business, headquartered in Salem, employed hundreds of local residents and contributed significantly to the city’s economy. After her death in 1883, her sons continued the business, maintaining the brand’s popularity for decades. The Lydia Pinkham Memorial Clinic, established in her honor, carries on her commitment to women’s health, providing services and resources to the Salem community.

The clinic’s location on Derby Street reflects Salem’s historic maritime past, a reminder that even during the city’s industrial boom, its connection to trade and commerce remained strong. Today, the Lydia Pinkham Memorial Clinic stands as a unique landmark, honoring a pioneering woman and continuing her legacy of care.
historical_note: The Lydia Pinkham Memorial Clinic at 250 Derby Street honors the legacy of Lydia Pinkham, a Salem resident who rose to prominence in the late 19th and early 20th centuries with her vegetable-based medicines for women's health. Pinkham’s remedies were widely marketed and distributed, making her a household name. The clinic, established in her memory, continues to provide health services to the Salem community.
historical_narration: Lydia Estes Pinkham was a pioneer in the development and marketing of herbal remedies for women’s health in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Born in Lynn, Massachusetts, she began creating a vegetable compound to alleviate the suffering of women during menstruation, pregnancy, and childbirth, and it quickly gained popularity. Pinkham established a manufacturing facility and mail-order business in Lynn, becoming a successful entrepreneur at a time when few women held such positions. Her remedies were widely advertised, and she built a substantial brand around her name and image, becoming a household name across the United States and Canada. The Lydia Pinkham Memorial Clinic in Salem was established to continue her work and provide healthcare services for women, serving as a lasting tribute to her contributions to women’s well-being and her innovative approach to health and business. The building itself, constructed in 1922, reflects the Colonial Revival architectural style popular at the time.

name: Salem Hospital
description: Salem Hospital, located at 81 Highland Avenue, was constructed in 1916 as a vital center for medical care. Designed by Edward A. Hoyt in the Classical Revival style, the building reflects the architectural trends of the early 20th century and continues to serve the community today. It is recognized by the Massachusetts Historical Commission as an inventoried property (SAL.4321).
short_narration: Built in 1916, Salem Hospital stands as a landmark of healthcare in the city, designed by architect Edward A. Hoyt in the Classical Revival style.
long_narration:
Salem Hospital has been a cornerstone of healthcare in Salem since its construction in 1916. Designed by architect Edward A. Hoyt, the building exemplifies the Classical Revival style, a popular choice for institutional buildings in the early 20th century, reflecting a sense of permanence and civic pride.

From its inception, the hospital was intended to address the growing medical needs of the Salem community. It quickly became a vital resource, providing essential care and contributing to the overall health and well-being of residents. Over the years, Salem Hospital has expanded its services and capabilities, evolving to meet the changing demands of modern medicine.

Today, as a member of Mass General Brigham, Salem Hospital continues to provide comprehensive care, offering a wide range of specialties and a commitment to exceptional quality. The building remains a significant landmark, recognized by the Massachusetts Historical Commission as an inventoried property (SAL.4321) and a testament to Salem’s dedication to healthcare.
historical_note: Look up at the pale brick and the orderly columns. This building, Salem Hospital, opened in 1916, a time when medical science was racing forward. Designed by architect Edward A. Hoyt in the Classical Revival style, it’s a deliberate echo of ancient Greece and Rome — a statement that medicine, like those empires, aspired to order and permanence. Before this, Salem’s medical care was scattered in smaller facilities. This was the first purpose-built hospital for the city, meant to centralize care and bring modern practices to the North Shore. Notice the symmetry of the windows, the clean lines. It’s not ornate, but it’s dignified. The building is inventoried by the Massachusetts Historical Commission, recognizing its contribution to the community. Even today, over a century later, the same dedication to care continues within those walls. Imagine the doctors and nurses who walked these halls during the Spanish Flu pandemic, or the soldiers returning from World War I who found healing here. The building still stands as a testament to Salem’s commitment to health and well-being.

name: Amelia Payson House
description: The Amelia Payson House, located at 16 Winter Street, was constructed in 1845 by Edward Payson for his wife, Amelia. Edward, a cashier at the Commercial Bank, built the home in the Greek Revival style, reflecting the architectural tastes of the era. Today, the Amelia Payson House operates as a highly-rated bed and breakfast, offering guests beautifully appointed rooms, gourmet breakfasts, and a touch of historic charm.
short_narration: Built in 1845 for Edward and Amelia Payson, this beautifully renovated Greek Revival home now welcomes guests as a refined bed and breakfast in the heart of Salem.
long_narration:
The Amelia Payson House stands as a testament to Salem's prosperous mid-19th century. Built in 1845 by Edward Payson, a prominent figure in Salem's banking community, the Greek Revival home was a symbol of the family's status and refined taste. Edward commissioned the house for himself and his wife, Amelia, creating a comfortable and elegant residence on Winter Street.

Over the years, the house has retained its historic character while undergoing careful renovations to provide modern comforts. Today, the Amelia Payson House welcomes guests as a beautifully appointed bed and breakfast, offering a luxurious experience with features like gas fireplaces and gourmet breakfasts. It has earned accolades from publications like Yankee Magazine and Fodor’s Travel, solidifying its reputation as one of Salem’s finest lodging establishments.

The Amelia Payson House offers a glimpse into Salem’s past, blending historic architecture with contemporary hospitality, and providing a memorable stay for visitors exploring the city’s rich history and vibrant culture.

long_narration: The Hawthorne Hotel has stood on Washington Square West since 1925, offering a blend of historic charm and modern hospitality. Named for Salem’s most famous literary son, Nathaniel Hawthorne, the hotel quickly became a landmark in the city, catering to visitors and locals alike. As a member of Historic Hotels of America, the Hawthorne Hotel is recognized for its preservation of Salem’s rich heritage and its commitment to providing a unique and memorable experience.\n\nThroughout the years, the Hawthorne Hotel has become known for more than just its luxurious accommodations. Stories of ghostly encounters have circulated for decades, earning the hotel a reputation as one of the most haunted places in Massachusetts. Whether you believe in the supernatural or not, the Hawthorne Hotel’s intriguing history and atmospheric ambiance are sure to captivate you. Today, the Hawthorne continues to be a premier destination for travelers seeking a taste of Salem’s history, hospitality, and perhaps, a glimpse of the other side.

name: Casey Lundregan Burns, P.C.
description: Casey Lundregan Burns, P.C. is a Salem law firm with a legacy stretching back to 1926. Founded by William J. Lundregan, the firm has been passed down through three generations, providing legal services in probate, estate planning, real estate, and elder law. Today, the firm continues to serve the community with a focus on protecting family assets and interests.
short_narration: Casey Lundregan Burns, P.C. has served Salem families with legal expertise in probate, estate planning, and elder law since 1926.
long_narration:
Casey Lundregan Burns, P.C. has deep roots in Salem, tracing its origins back to 1926 when William J. Lundregan established a practice after graduating law school. Originally combining legal work with his family’s bakery, Lundregan prioritized hard work and asset conservation, eventually serving as a State Representative and Presiding Justice of the Lynn District Court.

The firm continued to grow through the years, with William’s daughter, Pauline, joining the practice alongside her husband, Joseph David Casey. Later, their children, Pamela and Christopher, became partners, ensuring the firm remained family-owned and operated. In 2006, a merger with Lundregan and Lundregan consolidated two strong practices, solidifying the firm’s commitment to family asset protection.

Today, Casey Lundregan Burns, P.C. offers expertise in probate and estate litigation, estate planning, real estate law, elder law, and Medicaid planning. They remain dedicated to providing zealous advocacy and personalized legal services to families throughout Massachusetts.
historical_note: Casey Lundregan Burns, P.C. has been a trusted Salem law firm since 1926, offering comprehensive legal services in probate, estate planning, real estate, elder law, and trust litigation. Rooted in a family tradition of service, the firm has guided generations of families through complex legal matters, protecting their assets and interests. Today, the firm continues to build on its legacy of expertise and client-focused advocacy.

name: Bung Hole Liquor Store
description: The Bung Hole Liquor Store at 204 Derby Street occupies a space deeply rooted in Salem’s maritime history. The name “bung hole” refers to the plug used to fill a cask or barrel, a vital component in the storage and transport of goods like rum and molasses that fueled Salem’s trade in the 18th and 19th centuries. Today, the store offers a modern selection of spirits, but its location echoes a time when Derby Street bustled with the activity of shipwrights, merchants, and sailors.
short_narration: Located at 204 Derby Street, the Bung Hole Liquor Store continues a long tradition of maritime commerce on Salem's historic wharves.
long_narration: The Bung Hole Liquor Store, located at 204 Derby Street, draws its name from a nautical term referencing the plugs used to seal casks and barrels – essential for storing and transporting goods during Salem’s maritime heyday. Situated on Derby Street, a historic thoroughfare once teeming with wharves and trade, the store occupies a space that has long been connected to the flow of commerce and provisions. While now a modern liquor store, its name serves as a reminder of Salem’s rich seafaring past and the vital role of maritime trade in the city’s development.
historical_note: The Bung Hole Liquor Store at 204 Derby Street is a modern business with a name rooted in Salem’s maritime past. A ‘bung hole’ was a tap or opening drilled into a barrel to draw out its contents, a common sight along Salem’s busy wharves during the city’s heyday as a global trading port. While the store now offers a variety of spirits, its name serves as a quirky reminder of Salem’s seafaring heritage.

name: Circus Lane
description: Circus Lane, located at 10 Jefferson Avenue, is a unique antique and vintage shop offering a curated collection of goods, from fashion and collectibles to architectural salvage and art. The shop’s name and logo pay homage to Salem’s history as a stop for traveling circuses in the early 19th century, and to Old Bet, the first elephant brought to the United States by a Salem sea captain in 1796. Open Mondays, Thursdays, Fridays, and Saturdays, Circus Lane is a destination for those seeking one-of-a-kind treasures.
short_narration: Discover a treasure trove of vintage finds at Circus Lane, a Salem shop named for the historic path where circuses once performed and inspired by the story of Old Bet, America's first circus elephant.
long_narration:
Circus Lane isn't just a shop; it's a piece of Salem's colorful past. Located on what was once known as Circus Lane – a path bordering fields where traveling circuses set up in the early 1800s – the shop evokes a bygone era of spectacle and wonder. The name is a direct nod to this history, and the logo features Old Bet, the first elephant brought to America by a Salem shipping captain in 1796, a sensation that drew crowds and helped establish the American circus tradition.\n
Today, Circus Lane offers a diverse collection of vintage and antique goods, curated by a team of vendors from local markets like The Derby Square Flea and Pickering Wharf Antiques Gallery. You'll find everything from vintage fashion and collectibles to architectural salvage, jewelry, and handcrafted art, all reflecting the rich artistic traditions of New England. It's a place to browse, discover, and connect with Salem's creative spirit.

name: Essex Heritage
description: Essex Heritage Essex National Heritage Commission (Essex Heritage) is a non-profit organization that has been federally authorized to manage and oversee the unique heritage resources of the Essex National Heritage Area. Mission: To preserve and enhance the historic, cultural, and natural resources of the Essex National Heritage Area located in Essex County, Massachusetts. Vision: The historic, cultural and natural resources (and their associated history, organizations, and people) are recognized as critically important to maintaining the quality of life, community vitality, and economic sustainability of Essex County, Massachusetts. Essex National Heritage Area The Essex National Heritage Area was created as an act of Congress in 1996 and encompasses the 34 cities and towns of Essex County, Massachusetts. Within this NHA there are two National Parks (Salem Maritime National Historic Site and Saugus Ironworks National Historic Site) and the US Parker River National Wildlife Refuge. There are also several state parks, thousands of acres of conservation land, and numerous museums, historic houses and heritage sites that are open to the public and owned/managed by non-profit organizations and municipal governments. Land Acknowledgement: The Essex National Heritage Area encompasses land on which Native communities have thrived for millennia. The Pawtucket band of the Massachuset Tribe inhabited the land that is now known as Essex County. As advocates for the appropriate interpretation and stewardship of our many natural, historic, and cultural resources, Essex Heritage has a responsibility to acknowledge all stories, past and present.
short_narration: Essex Heritage. Mission: To preserve and enhance the historic, cultural, and natural resources of the Essex National Heritage Area located in Essex County, Massachusetts

H&H Propeller Shop has been a vital part of Salem’s working waterfront since 1950. Originally established to serve the needs of local boaters and the commercial fishing fleet, the shop quickly gained a reputation for expertise in marine driveline components – propellers, shafts, and related hardware.

Recently reopened under new ownership, H&H Propeller has expanded its workshop capabilities while retaining the skilled craftsmen who have long been the backbone of the business. They offer a full range of services, from routine maintenance and repair to custom fabrication and performance optimization, serving recreational boaters, marinas, and even government vessels.

Located on Essex Street, H&H Propeller continues a tradition of maritime craftsmanship that has been essential to Salem’s seafaring heritage for generations.

name: Lifebridge Thrift Shop
description: Lifebridge Northshore is a non-profit organization dedicated to addressing homelessness and food insecurity in the North Shore region of Massachusetts. Founded in 1978 as a soup kitchen run by The Crombie Street Congregational Church, Lifebridge has grown to offer a range of services and hosts events like 'Breaking Bread, Building Hope' and 'Together for Women' to raise awareness and funds. Their thrift shop at 47 Canal Street provides affordable goods while supporting their mission.
short_narration: Lifebridge Northshore has been serving the North Shore community since 1978, providing vital support to individuals and families experiencing homelessness and food insecurity.
long_narration: Lifebridge Northshore began its vital work in 1978, originating from a commitment by The Crombie Street Congregational Church to provide outreach to Salem’s homeless population. What started as a small soup kitchen serving meals two evenings a week has evolved into a comprehensive organization offering year-round support to individuals and families facing homelessness and food insecurity.\n\nToday, Lifebridge Northshore operates a thrift shop at 47 Canal Street, offering affordable furniture, clothing, and household goods to the community. Proceeds from the shop directly fund their programs and services. They also host a variety of events throughout the year, such as ‘Breaking Bread, Building Hope’ to address food insecurity, and ‘Together for Women’ to support vulnerable women. Lifebridge’s ongoing commitment ensures that those in need on the North Shore have access to essential resources and a path towards stability.

name: Punto Urban Art Museum
description: The Punto Urban Art Museum is a unique open-air museum in Salem, born from the mission of North Shore CDC, a community development non-profit founded in 1978. The museum features 75 large-scale murals by 40 global and 25 New England-based artists, aiming to break down socio-economic barriers and foster community through public art. Visitors can explore the murals and learn about the artists and the social justice themes they explore.
short_narration: The Punto Urban Art Museum transforms Salem’s streets into an open-air gallery, showcasing 75 large-scale murals created by artists from around the world.
long_narration:
The Punto Urban Art Museum is more than just an outdoor gallery; it’s a testament to the power of art to build community and address social issues. Created by North Shore CDC in 1978, the museum began as a way to revitalize neighborhoods and foster a sense of belonging through public art. Today, it encompasses 75 large-scale murals spread throughout the city, each a vibrant expression of the artist’s vision and a reflection of the community’s spirit.

The museum’s collection features a diverse range of artists, both local and international, bringing a global perspective to Salem’s streets. The murals aren’t simply decorative; they’re designed to spark conversation, raise awareness about social justice issues, and promote equity. Educational tours offer deeper insights into the artwork and the stories behind it, connecting visitors to the artists and the communities they represent.

By transforming ordinary walls into extraordinary canvases, the Punto Urban Art Museum invites visitors to experience Salem in a new light, celebrating its diversity, resilience, and commitment to social change. It’s a living, breathing museum that continues to evolve and inspire.
historical_note: Founded in 1978 as part of the North Shore CDC’s community development work, the Punto Urban Art Museum is a unique open-air museum in Salem. It features 75 large-scale murals by over 65 artists, aiming to break down socio-economic barriers and foster community through public art. The museum offers educational tours exploring themes of social justice, equity, and the history of Salem’s neighborhoods.

name: Destination Salem

Destination Salem serves as the official destination marketing organization for the City of Salem, Massachusetts. Established as a non-profit, public-private partnership, it cooperatively promotes Salem as a unique and enriching destination for a diverse range of travelers, from families and couples to international visitors and those interested in American history and culture.

The organization is funded through a combination of city support and investments from local businesses who advertise in the annual Salem City Guide & Map and the Guide to Haunted Happenings. This collaborative model ensures that Destination Salem’s efforts are aligned with the needs of both the tourism industry and the Salem community.

Destination Salem’s mission extends beyond simply attracting visitors. For over 25 years, Salem Historical Tours has been sharing the stories of Salem with visitors from around the world. Established in 1997, this family-owned and operated company is the oldest professional independent walking tour provider in the Witch City, offering a wide array of experiences that delve into Salem’s complex and captivating history.

Their tours cover a broad spectrum of topics, from the infamous 1692 Witch Trials and Salem’s maritime heritage to the city’s Revolutionary War connections and its enduring reputation for paranormal activity. Guides are passionate about Salem’s past and committed to providing accurate and engaging narratives, ensuring that each tour is both informative and entertaining.

Salem Historical Tours is dedicated to providing authentic experiences, offering a unique way to connect with the history and atmosphere of this iconic New England city. name: Salem Smugglers' Tour
description: The Salem Smugglers' Tour offers a unique perspective on Salem's past, taking visitors beneath the streets to explore a network of tunnels dating back to the 17th and 18th centuries. Originally used for storage and drainage, these tunnels became notorious for illicit activity during the Revolutionary War and beyond. Today, the tour reveals the fascinating stories of smugglers, pirates, and the everyday citizens who navigated this hidden world.
short_narration: Delve into Salem's hidden history with the Salem Smugglers' Tour, exploring the city's network of secret tunnels and the stories of those who used them.
long_narration:
The Salem Smugglers' Tour invites you to descend into the shadowy underbelly of Salem, exploring a labyrinth of tunnels that once lay hidden beneath the bustling streets. These passages, originally constructed for practical purposes like drainage and storage in the 17th and 18th centuries, soon attracted a more clandestine clientele. During the Revolutionary War, they became vital routes for smugglers evading British taxes, and later served as hideouts for pirates and a haven for illicit trade.\n
The tour guides share captivating tales of the individuals who frequented these tunnels – merchants, sailors, and those seeking to circumvent the law. Discover how these hidden pathways shaped Salem’s economy and contributed to its reputation as a center of commerce and intrigue. The tour offers a unique glimpse into a side of Salem rarely seen, revealing the secrets that lie beneath the historic streets.
historical_note: The Salem Smugglers' Tour offers a unique perspective on Salem's rich history, taking visitors beneath the city streets to explore a network of tunnels. These passages, dating back to the 17th and 18th centuries, were originally built for drainage and trade, but quickly became notorious for illicit activities. Today, the tour reveals the stories of merchants, pirates, and the hidden underbelly of this historic port town.

Unlike many tours that focus solely on sensationalism, The Salem Witch Walk emphasizes historical accuracy and empathy for those impacted by the 1692 witch trials. The Salem Witch Walk provides a respectful and informative experience for those seeking a deeper understanding of this pivotal moment in American history.
